coredata.dc:title: Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based dual functional composite films integrated with metal-organic frameworks and anthocyanin for active and intelligent food packaging
coredata.dc:creator: Riahi, Zohreh
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: Innovative active and pH-colorimetric composite films were fabricated from gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) (Gel/PVA) integrated with copper-based metal-organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) and red cabbage anthocyanin (RCA). The incorporation of Cu-MOFs improved the tensile strength, water resistance, and UV shielding properties of the developed composite films. The addition of anthocyanins and 3 wt% Cu-MOFs endowed the polymer matrix with excellent antioxidant (100 % against ABTS and DPPH radicals) and antibacterial (against Gram-positive and Gram-negative foodborne pathogenic bacteria) functions. The fabricated composite films exhibited significant color change at alkaline conditions of pH 7–12 and a marked color change upon exposure to ammonia. The designed indicator films used for shrimp freshness tracking and a visual color change from pink (for fresh shrimp) to green (for spoiled shrimp) was observed during storage at 28 °C for 24 h. The potential applications of the engineered composite films were studied by shrimp packaging, and the quality parameters of packaged samples were monitored during storage. The synergistic effects of adding anthocyanins and MOF nanostructures works for better product freshness preservation and responds well to shrimp spoilage level, introducing novel active and intelligent packaging options for practical smart packaging applications.

head.ce:title: Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based dual functional composite films integrated with metal-organic frameworks and anthocyanin for active and intelligent food packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Innovative active and pH-colorimetric composite films were fabricated from gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) (Gel/PVA) integrated with copper-based metal-organic frameworks (Cu-MOFs) and red cabbage anthocyanin (RCA). The incorporation of Cu-MOFs improved the tensile strength, water resistance, and UV shielding properties of the developed composite films. The addition of anthocyanins and 3 wt% Cu-MOFs endowed the polymer matrix with excellent antioxidant (100 % against ABTS and DPPH radicals) and antibacterial (against Gram-positive and Gram-negative foodborne pathogenic bacteria) functions. The fabricated composite films exhibited significant color change at alkaline conditions of pH 7–12 and a marked color change upon exposure to ammonia. The designed indicator films used for shrimp freshness tracking and a visual color change from pink (for fresh shrimp) to green (for spoiled shrimp) was observed during storage at 28 °C for 24 h. The potential applications of the engineered composite films were studied by shrimp packaging, and the quality parameters of packaged samples were monitored during storage. The synergistic effects of adding anthocyanins and MOF nanostructures works for better product freshness preservation and responds well to shrimp spoilage level, introducing novel active and intelligent packaging options for practical smart packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: A significant amount of food is wasted in the supply chain each year, and reports suggest that approximately 60 % of food loss is preventable [1]. Proper food packaging significantly reduces food waste due to spoilage during storage and distribution. There is increasing research interest in active packaging films, including antibacterial and antioxidant films [2,3]. These films are attracting attention for their potential to enhance food safety and extend shelf life by inhibiting the growth of foodborne microorganisms and preventing the oxidation of nutrients. A variety of active compounds including natural plant extracts, food sources, essential oils, and nanomaterials, are applied to manufacture these active packaging systems [4].

A recent review has shown that metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) can be potential active substances in active food packaging applications [5,6]. MOFs have emerged as a unique subset of porous nanomaterials formed by linking metal ions and organic ligand clusters via coordinate bonds [7]. MOFs exhibit beneficial properties such as controllable pore size, high surface area, functional flexibility, easy modification, and good biocompatibility [8]. A variety of preparation processes, such as mechanochemical, hydrothermal, electrochemical, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted methods, are used to make these materials for catalysis, sensors, tissue engineering, gas/moisture capture, and drug delivery applications [9–11]. These novel hybrid materials appear promising for food packaging technology due to their excellent functional properties, such as ethylene/oxygen scavenging, antibacterial/antioxidant activity, and UV protection [10,12].

The physicochemical and functional characteristics of MOFs can depend on the choice of ligands and metal ions. Typically, ligands used in MOF synthesis have multiple carboxyl or amine functional groups extending from either an alkyl chain or ring-based structures such as benzene or imidazole [13]. 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) is a rigid planar molecule with two carboxylate moieties arranged at a fixed angle of 120°. It contains six oxygen atoms that can act as coordination sites providing different coordination modes for interacting with the central metal. It has been widely used in the synthesis of multidimensional metal-organic networks due to its favorable crosslinking and oxygen donating properties [14]. Zirconium(IV), iron(III), zinc(II), and copper(II) are the predominant metal ions commonly used in MOFs designed for various biological applications [13]. Among them, Cu-based MOFs have attracted particular attention in recent research as a flexible platform for various biomedical purposes, either independently or as part of MOFs-embedded composites, due to the inherent antibacterial properties of Cu2+ [15]. For example, Gwon et al. [16] developed hydrogels based on Cu-, Co-, and Zn-MOFs. The antibacterial efficacy of these hydrogels against E. coli and S. aureus showed a trend of Cu-MOFs > Co-MOFs > Zn-MOFs. There are reports on the application of copper-based MOF particles to poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) [17], pectin [18], and poly(lactic acid) (PLA) to create bacteriocidal composites [19].

Nonetheless, these bioactive packaging films lack the ability to monitor food freshness in a timely manner, preventing consumers from selecting products based on their preferred level of freshness [20]. The dynamic nature of the food market is driving demand for advanced packaging technologies that can go beyond food protection and monitor food quality in real-time. Therefore, there is pressure to design dual-function active and intelligent (smart) packaging films that combine the ability to preserve food freshness and provide real-time monitoring [21].

In recent years, innovative packaging systems based on pH-responsive color-changing materials have attracted great interest due to their ability to quickly and easily detect changes in food conditions [22]. pH-responsive freshness-indicating films contain natural pigments, usually derived from plants. During storage, these pigments change color in response to pH changes due to microbial and chemical activity, allowing real-time food quality monitoring [23]. In particular, anthocyanins are widely used in the production of pH-responsive packaging materials [20]. Anthocyanins derived from red cabbage (Brassica oleracea) sources have been widely recommended for smart packaging manufacturing compared to anthocyanins derived from other natural sources due to their cost effectiveness, availability, abundance, and reliable halochromic properties [24].

In contrast to synthetic polymers, intelligent packaging films utilize natural degradable polymers like chitosan, pectin, gelatin, CNF (cellulose nanofibrils), agar, and κ-carrageenan. Among these, gelatin is a natural protein-based hydrophilic polymer derived from collagen found in animal connective tissue. It has found extensive application in the field of food packaging due to its excellent film properties, richness, safety, and gas barrier properties [25]. However, hydrophilicity (high moisture transfer and low water resistance), poor mechanical properties, and lack of antibacterial/antioxidant properties can be problematic in food packaging applications [26]. One effective strategy is to improve the formulation of film preparations and create binary composite films. Min et al. [27] reported that blend films composed of gelatin and PVA in a 3:1 weight ratio demonstrated favorable physical characteristics. PVA is an eco-friendly bioplastic that has gained attention due to its outstanding chemical resistance, film-forming properties, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [28]. It is a semi-crystalline polymer synthesized by the hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate, which has a variety of uses in the food industry [29].

This work aimed to design a new eco-friendly colorimetric composite film with food protection potential by incorporating Cu-MOFs and anthocyanins into a Gel/PVA blend film. MOF nanoparticles were used as active compounds that exhibit some functional properties, such as antioxidant and antibacterial activity, and red cabbage anthocyanin (RCA) was used as a pH-sensitive indicator. Meanwhile, MOF nanostructures with a high surface area can act as an adsorbent filler to concentrate volatile organic amines and enhance the freshness monitoring efficacy of the developed indicator by enhancing color change.

To this end, Gel/PVA-based composite films were prepared by adding Cu-MOFs and RCA, and the effect of the fillers on the chemical structure, microstructure, and UV resistance of the prepared composite films was investigated. In addition, the mechanical properties, antibacterial efficacy, antioxidant activity and pH-responsive color change of these composite films were also investigated. Finally, packaging tests (shrimp packaging) were conducted to test the efficacy of the composite films for smart packaging applications.

Type A food-grade gelatin was purchased from Gel-Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, South Korea). 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) and copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O) were procured from TCI America (Portland, OR, USA) and Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Seoul, South Korea), respectively. Fresh shrimp (refrigerated at 4 °C with four days validity from the purchase date) and red cabbage were bought from a local market in Seoul, South Korea. Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313) and Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895) were obtained from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC). Poly(vinyl alcohol) (~99 % hydrolyzed and MW of 146,000–186,000 kDa), 2,2′-Azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Cu-MOFs and red cabbage anthocyanin
ce:section.ce:para: Anthocyanin was extracted from red cabbage (Brassica oleracea L.) by following a procedure described in the published literature [31]. 100 g of dry red cabbage powder was ground and soaked in 1000 mL of a 1:4 water/ethanol mixture. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting solution was vacuum filtered, and the supernatant was concentrated in the dark using a rotary evaporator at 40 °C. Afterward, extracts were stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

To make a film solution, 3 g of gelatin, 1 g of PVA, and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water with constant stirring at 80 °C for 60 min. The solution was cooled down to 55 °C and stirred for 15 min while adding 10 mL of RCA. Then, various concentrations of Cu-MOFs (1 and 3 wt% based on the polymers) were added, and the mixture was vigorously stirred for 15 min. The solution was then cast onto Teflon-coated plates and dried at 23 ± 2 °C for 48 h. The dried films were peeled off and stored in a 25 °C/50 % relative humidity (RH) environment until further testing. In addition, the control film was prepared by blending two polymers without any filler. The fabricated composite films were coded Gel/PVA, Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1%, and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3%, according to the filler concentration. A detailed description of the methods used for physicochemical characterization and evaluation of the film's functional properties are provided in Supporting Information.

The sensitivity of the indicator films was tested through a simulation study using acid and base gases [32]. The indicator was labeled over a conical flask filled with 30 mL of acetic acid (99 %) or ammonia (0.8 M) for 15 min. The color response of the tested films was noted using a chroma meter (CR-400, Konica-Minolta, Tokyo, Japan).

Real-time quality monitoring during the storage of shrimp, a perishable food product, was studied. The circular discs (diameter: 3 cm) of developed indicator film were attached to the inside of a PS (polystyrene) container containing 100 g of shrimp samples at 28 °C for 1 day. The color changes of indicator films were determined every 12 h intervals using a colorimeter.

The pH of the stored samples was tested after homogenizing 5 g of sample with 45 mL of distilled water and measuring the pH of the resulting slurry using a digital pH meter (A-211-pH meter, Thermo Scientific, Indonesia).

Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) concentration was determined using a steam distillation method. The TVB-N was extracted using a Kjeldahl distillation unit, collected in a conical flask, and subsequently subjected to titration using 0.1N sulfuric acid to quantify the amount of evolved TVB-N. The TVB-N concentration is reported in mg/100 g sample (mg%).

A packaging test was conducted to evaluate the dual function of the film in terms of extending the shelf life of shrimp and real-time monitoring of freshness. Briefly, 25 g of fresh shrimp samples were wrapped in sterile film (10 cm × 10 cm) of different groups, placed in a low-density polyethylene (LDPE) bag, vacuum-sealed, and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. During the storage period, changes in shrimp quality (color, pH, and total bacterial count) were observed at weekly intervals (day 0, 7, 14, and 21). Photographic images of the indicator film were obtained using a digital camera (Samsung Electronics, Seoul, South Korea). After opening the bag, the pH of digested product was measured using a digital pH meter. In addition, 100 μL of the slurry was inoculated on an agar plate to evaluate the total viable cell count (TVC) to confirm the bactericidal power of the prepared packaging film. Samples were tested in triplicate, and the average value was reported.

The quality characteristics of each produced film were evaluated three times, and the results were reported as means±SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was applied, and Duncan's multiple range test using the SPSS statistical analysis program (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) evaluated the significant differences between test groups at the 95 % confidence level.

Cu-MOF nanostructures were synthesized via a solvothermal method. The morphology of the Cu-MOFs was examined by the FE-SEM images (Fig. 2a,b), revealing numerous uniform flower-like crystals composed of nanosheets. The obtained blue flower-like MOFs present a larger specific surface area, high porosity, and more exposed active sites [33], proposing a good bioactive material with excellent functional properties for food packaging applications. In addition, the large surface area of the MOFs contributes to an improved absorption capacity for volatile alkaline gases and the sensitivity of the composite films for detecting volatile amines [34].

The FT-IR spectrum of the prepared Cu-MOFs (Fig. 2c) shows that the asymmetric stretching of the carboxylic group is present at 1614 cm−1, 1429 cm−1, and 1386 cm−1 [35]. The Cu substitution on benzene groups is confirmed by the peak at 740 cm−1. The stretching vibration peak of the C–O–Cu bond in the crystals of MOFs presented an absorption peak of about 986 cm−1 [36]. The broad peak of about 3398 cm−1 is attributed to water molecules bound between the Cu-MOFs [35]. Therefore, the stretching of the entire Cu-MOF ring was reflected by strong peaks near 1350–1500 cm−1, whereas in-plane bending of the ring was indicated by multiple peaks in the spectral range of 900–1350 cm−1 [37].

The XRD diffraction pattern of the Cu-MOFs is shown in Fig. 2d. Cu-MOFs demonstrated distinct diffraction peaks at 7.2, 10.2, 12.5, 14.6, 16.3, 19.4, 26.2, and 40.1°, corresponding to the (200), (220), (222), (400), (420), (511), (440), (731) and (882) crystal planes. The narrow and sharp peaks confirm the crystalline nature of prepared MOF nanostructures, consistent with the reported literature [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial activity of MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant capacity, specifically the ability to effectively neutralize free radicals, is a crucial characteristic of active packaging due to the potential for free radicals to induce oxidative harm and food spoilage [38]. The radical scavenging ability of the MOF nanostructures against DPPH and ABTS free radicals is shown in Fig. 2e. The particles showed dose-dependent antiradical activities. The MOF nanostructures exhibited antioxidant activities of 21.2 % and 10.5 % against ABTS and DPPH, respectively, at 25 μg/mL. When the particle concentration increased from 25 to 300 μg/mL, the radical scavenging capacity of MOFs was significantly enhanced (p < 0.05), showing 100 % and 71.5 % for ABTS and DPPH. The radical scavenging ability of MOFs is attributed to edge site features such as -NH2, -OH, and -COOH, which exhibit H-donating behavior. In addition, donating electrons by MOFs aids in antiradical activity by forming the adduct [8]. This redox function of MOFs can be important in neutralizing free radicals, quenching singlet and triplet oxygen, or decomposing superoxides. The radical neutralization ability of MOF nanostructures and their practical potential as active fillers in active food packaging technology has been previously reported [39,40].

The antibacterial act capacity of MOF nanostructures against Gram-positive (L. monocytogenes and S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli and S. enterica) foodborne pathogens was evaluated by measuring MIC/MBC values and inhibition zone, and the results are shown in Supplementary Table S1 and Fig. 2f, respectively. The MIC of Cu-MOFs was 700 and 300 μg/mL for E. coli and S. enterica, and 200 and 100 μg/mL for L. monocytogenes and S. aureus, respectively (Table S1). In addition, the diffusion and dispersion of MOF nanostructures exerted a potent inhibitory effect on the growth of all tested bacteria, as shown in the agar well diffusion test. The MOFs exhibited superior antibacterial action against Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria, and the MIC and diffusion test results are in close agreement. The MOFs exhibited a stronger antibacterial effect against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus with larger inhibition zones, consistent with the MIC test results showing lower MIC values. A possible reason for the difference in the antibacterial activity of Cu-MOFs against these microorganisms is the difference in the cell membrane structure of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and the binding ability of MOFs to these bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer without a distinct outer membrane. The lack of an outer cell wall in Gram-positive bacteria helps foreign particles to diffuse by the cell wall. However, the outer membrane in Gram-negative bacteria can block MOFs from entering the cell [41].

The outstanding antibacterial activity of copper-based MOF nanostructures is ascribed to the following factors: (i) Copper ions and ligands released from the MOF skeleton can wreck the integrity of cell membrane, thereby causing morphological change, increasing membrane permeability and causing the leakage of internal nutrients and other contents, finally kill bacteria [42]. (ii) The active sites on the surface of the metal-organic frameworks facilitate the degradation of proteins and fatty acids located on the bacterial membrane. The large surface area of MOF nanostructures intensifies the interaction of particles and surfaces' bacterial membranes, enhancing the bactericidal functions of particles. (iii) Particles and metal ions can interact with the nucleus and damage DNA or proteases when they enter a cell, leading to cell death or losing the ability to divide and proliferate. (iv) Copper ions can induce cells to produce intracellular ROS (reactive oxygen species), further disrupting the synthesis of DNA and amino acids, accelerating the rupture of bacterial cell walls and inducing bacterial death [43]. The complex chemical effects of copper ions mentioned above and the physical damage of particle structural factors can synergize to kill bacteria.

The microstructure of the Gel/PVA-based composite films with different contents of Cu-MOFs was observed using the FE-SEM images (Fig. 3a,b). The surface of Gel/PVA showed a uniform appearance without cavities and cracks, indicating that gelatin and PVA were mixed evenly. The internal structure of the Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% composite film was very regular and uniform. The surface of the Gel/PVA film was roughened with the addition of 3 wt% filler. The superior blending of MOFs within the polymer matrix leads to a denser film network structure, resulting in excellent compatibility and miscibility with the polymer matrix. The continuous cross-sections of the composite films without any pore formation also confirmed the good distribution of MOFs in the polymer matrix (Fig. 3b). The added MOFs can cross-link with the blended biopolymer matrix through reactive groups on the filler surface, such as amino hydroxyl groups that can form hydrogen bonds with the polymer matrix [44]. As a result, the incorporation of MOF fillers into the Gel/PVA polymer matrix resulted in composite films with distinct and well-defined shapes and structures, suggesting that the physical properties of composite films could be improved.

The FT-IR spectra of the biocomposite films reinforced with Cu-MOF particles and anthocyanin are shown in Fig. 3c. The transmittance peaks at 3279 cm−1 and 2936 cm−1 can be assigned to the O−H and C−H stretching of the Gel and PVA chains. The CO stretching of amide-I, N−H bending of amide-II, and C−N & N−H stretching of amide-III in the gelatin, presented characteristic peaks at 1634, 1548, and 1238cm−1, respectively [25]. The peak around 1445cm−1 and 1086cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration modes of -CH2 and C-O-C stretching of the PVA polymer, respectively [45]. The distinct peaks observed in Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% coincided with the corresponding stretching vibrations of -OH, C−H, CO, C−N, and N−H present in the Gel/PVA polymer blend. Compared to the Gel/PVA film as a reference, no noticeable shifts or alterations in peak intensity and positions were observed by including MOFs and anthocyanin, suggesting no significant chemical modifications within the composite films. The bonding between the fillers and the Gel/PVA polymer blend can be attributed to physical interactions, including hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces [46].

XPS investigation determined the elemental compositions and chemical state of Gel/PVA and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films. The survey spectra of the XPS result (Fig. 3d) show that Gel/PVA and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% are primarily comprised of C (carbon), N (nitrogen), and O (oxygen) as the main constitutional elements. Three distinct strong characteristic peaks at 284.9, 399.6, and 530.5 eV match the binding energies of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. There was no noticeable distinction in the deconvoluted spectra between the pure Gel/PVA and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films. However, a peak was observed at 931.8 eV in the Gel/PVA film loaded with 3 wt% Cu-MOFs, corresponding to Cu 2p‌. Fig. 3e illustrates that the deconvoluted Cu 2p spectrum possessed two peaks located around 931.9 and 951.8 eV, indicated to the Cu 2p3/2 core levels with Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 splitting, respectively. Fig. 3f shows that the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s can be split into four fitting peaks positioned around 283.7, 284.9, 287.0, and 288.1 eV, reflecting the C-H/C-C, C=C/C-N, C-O, and CO bonding structures, respectively [47]. For the deconvoluted spectrum of O 1s, the component peaks positioned around 530.2 eV, 531.2 eV, and 532.3 eV reflect the presence of CO, C-O-C/C-O-H and O-C-O groups, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3g. The Fig. 3h depicts the fitting curve for the N 1s, which can be fragmented into three components peaks attributed to the bonding structure of (397.6 eV) C-N-C, (398.6 eV) N(C)3, and (399.3 eV) N−H groups [48]. Therefore, the XPS spectrum analysis validated the successful loading of fillers to the Gel/PVA polymer structure [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface color and optical properties of Gel/PVA/MOFs films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a shows the appearance of the created Gel/PVA-based composite films. The virgin Gel/PVA film was colorless and transparent. In comparison, the films appeared to have a pink/red color when anthocyanin and MOFs were added to the polymer matrix due to the red color of anthocyanin and the blue color of MOFs. Combining anthocyanin and 3 wt% MOFs resulted in slightly different hues than the Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1%. The large surface area of MOFs enhances colorant adsorption, enhancing the synergistic effect of mixing the two colors to produce a darker color. The color of composite films was uniform, indicating that anthocyanin and MOF particles were evenly distributed throughout the polymer matrix. Table 1 displays the numerical values for the surface colors of the composite films. The neat Gel/PVA film exhibited a high L-value (91.4 ± 0.2) and low a-value (4.1 ± 0.01) and b-value (0.7 ± 0.02) owing to its transparent and colorless characteristics. Nevertheless, the incorporation of MOFs and anthocyanins into the film resulted in reduced brightness (L-value), accompanied by an increase in redness (a-value) and blueness (b-value) due to the red and blue color of RCA and Cu-MOFs, respectively [11,16]. As a result, the overall color difference (ΔE) of the composite films also increased significantly (p < 0.05) depending on the particle concentration.

The degradation of food quality and the oxidation and formation of harmful substances can occur due to the breakdown of nutrients and bioactive compounds in food caused by light exposure [32]. Thus, food packaging materials are crucial in blocking different light wavelengths to ensure food protection. The light transmittance of a film is an essential parameter that indicates its transparency or UV-shielding ability [50]. Fig. 4b displays the light transmittance spectra of the fabricated composite films from 200 to 800 nm wavelength. The neat Gel/PVA film did not show a pronounced light absorption. Incorporating RCA and MOFs imparted ultraviolet and visible light absorption peaks, depending on the concentration. The pure Gel/PVA film exhibited high transmission of visible light (T660
~ 87.4 %) and low transmission of ultraviolet light (T280
~ 20.2 %). The aromatic amino acids in gelatin molecules absorb UV light, presenting a low T280 value for pure Gel/PVA film [51].

In contrast, PVA films show high transparency to UV light, and a T280 value of 51 % has been reported for neat PVA films [52]. After adding the filler, the visible light transmission properties of the composite films were significantly changed, and the transparency decreased significantly (p < 0.05) to 81.1 % and 67.2 % incorporating 1 and 3 wt% MOF nanostructures. On the other hand, 100 % UV blocking property was observed for Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% due to the intense UV absorption of MOF and RCA particles. The UV absorption of Cu-MOFs can be related to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of CO bonds and the electronic transition of MOF's aromatic rings [53]. Meanwhile, some researchers documented T280 reduction of anthocyanins due to UV absorption by their phenolic compounds [54]. The results demonstrate that MOFs can enhance the UV protection of polymeric films with less sacrifice of the film's visibility and open promising new avenues for use in food packaging.

The mechanical properties of food packaging films, such as durability and ability to withstand external pressure, are very important throughout the storage, transportation, and handling of packaged food [55]. The effect of the incorporation of anthocyanins and MOFs on the mechanical properties of the composite films was investigated in terms of tensile strength (TS), modulus of elasticity (EM), and elongation at break (EB). The stress-strain curve of the composite films and quantitive values of TS, EM, and EB are presented in Fig. 5a and Table S2, respectively. The thickness of the Gel/PVA film increased significantly (p < 0.05) from 48.2 ± 1.7 μm to 66.8 ± 3.1 and 77.3 ± 2.3 μm when the nanofillers were added. The Gel/PVA film was relatively strong, with TS of ~65.7 MPa. The addition of fillers significantly increased (p<0.05) the TS of the Gel/PVA films. However, the stiffness (EM) and flexibility (EB) of the composite films did not change significantly (p > 0.05). The interfacial interactions between the high surface MOF and the polymer backbone and the formation of intramolecular and intermolecular H-bonding between them are responsible for the strengthening effect of the MOFs, enhancing the breaking energy requirement [56]. In addition, the presence of MOF nanostructures enhances the strength of the films by confining the motion of molecular chains in the composite films by occupying the interchain space in the polymer blend backbone [57]. The tensile strength and Young's modulus of the composite films were much greater than those of commercial polyethylene films (35.13 MPa and 113.25 MPa, respectively) [58]. Similar effects of additives on the mechanical characteristics of several bionanocomposite films have been reported [59–61]. In addition, the water-resistance improvement of the composite films is discussed in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of the composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Food oxidation is a chain reaction that generates free radicals, leading to rancidity, nutrient oxidation, discoloration, and off-flavor formation, reducing food quality and shelf life [29]. Hence, the inclusion of antioxidants in food packaging materials can extend the storage life of food. The antioxidant efficacy of the fabricated films was determined by measuring the DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity in direct contact with films (Fig. 5b). The neat Gel/PVA film showed negligible antioxidant activity (~5 % against ABTS and 1 % for DPPH). The DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity of Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% were 73.5 ± 1.1 % and 100 %, respectively. In particular, when the content of MOFs increased to 3wt%, the color of the DPPH (deep violet) and ABTS solution (cyan blue) was changed to a light yellow and colorless solution, showing 100 % radical scavenging capacity.

The radicals of ABTS and DPPH can be quenched through the combined effects of hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and radical adduct generation provided by MOFs. Furthermore, the promoted antioxidant of Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF film is possibly due to the synergistic antioxidant effect of red cabbage anthocyanin and MOF fillers, and the effect is positively correlated with the concentration. Several studies have reported that red cabbage anthocyanin solutions can exert antioxidant activity on the biopolymer matrix due to the high phenolic compound contents [62]. In addition, the hydrophilicity of the Gel/PVA film facilitates the release of the active ingredient into the ABTS aqueous solution, enhancing the interactions between active fillers and ABTS free radicals [63].

Antibacterial food packaging helps keep food safe, extend shelf life, reduce food waste, and ensure overall food quality and freshness by inhibiting the growth of foodborne microorganisms [64]. The antibacterial efficiency of the Gel/PVA-based films against L. monocytogenes and E. coli is shown in Fig. 6a,b. The Gel/PVA film did not exhibit any inhibitory effect against the tested bacteria, indicating that the polymer matrix lacked antibacterial properties against these pathogenic strains. However, the Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films exhibited bactericidal effects towards both tested bacteria and completely prevented the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli. It has been reported that the addition of MOF nanostructures imparted strong antibacterial activity to various biopolymer-based composite films, such as pectin [18], pullulan/PVA [65], and PVA/starch [66] films. In addition, Mahmoudi et al. [67] reported that the red cabbage anthocyanin had MIC/MBC values of 100/200 mg/mL, which were significantly higher than the MIC/MBC values observed for MOF particles. Therefore, the powerful antibacterial activities of the composite films mostly come from the MOFs. However, the synergetic effects of the antibacterial properties of the Cu-MOFs fillers and RCA can improve the functional properties of the films. The strong antibacterial ability of Cu-MOFs suggests that they can be used in food preservation applications in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color change of RCA solution and indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structures and color of anthocyanin can change in buffer solutions with different pH values [68]. The color variation of red cabbage anthocyanin (RCA) in buffer solutions at pH 2–12 was evaluated (Fig. 7a). The color of RCA at pH 2–6, 7–10, and 11–12 was red, purple, and green, respectively. The structural transformation of anthocyanins caused the color change of RCA from red flavonoid cations to purple quinonoidal anhydrobase, blue quinoidal, and light yellow chalcone [69]. Similar results were obtained in other anthocyanin-rich extracts [69].

The pH sensitivity of anthocyanins causes films containing these compounds to exhibit evident color changes when exposed to varying pH levels. The pH response of the films containing RCA and MOF fillers over the pH range from 2 to 12 and the corresponding color parameters (L, a, b, and ΔE) are shown in Fig. 7b,d. Both composite films displayed a similar pattern to the RCA solution, demonstrating obvious color variation (shifting from red/pink to green) as the pH of the buffer solution increased from 2 to 12. Other researchers also reported the films containing RCA exhibited the same color change trend under different buffer solutions [69–71]. Overall, the a-value decreased gradually to a negative value with the pH increasing from 2 to 12, indicating color switching from red to green. Furthermore, the b-value fluctuated from positive to negative, confirming the occurrence of chartreuse colors in composite films. The changes in the color value a and b were similar to the results reported by Chen et al. [72]. At the same pH, the color of Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films darker than Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% may be due to improved buffer solution adsorption caused by the increased amount of MOFs [34]. Typically, the pH of seafood products during the spoilage process falls within the range of 7–8 [73]. The composite films displayed noticeable changes in color within this range, indicating that color alterations can be easily perceived by the naked eye. These findings validate the capability of Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF films to serve as pH-sensitive indicators for monitoring food freshness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to ammonia and acid vapors
ce:section.ce:para: Several volatile compounds are generated during the breakdown of muscle proteins, such as ammonia, amines, sulfides, and others. Among these compounds, volatile nitrogen substances, especially ammonia, contribute to increased pH levels in muscle-based food products [32]. Ammonia-responsive films possess the potential to serve as intelligent packaging materials that can signal the spoilage of high-protein meat products. A simulation food deterioration study was conducted to test the gas sensor capability through the color change of Gel/PVA-based films to ammonia and acid vapors. Fig. 7c compares the color response behavior of virgin Gel/PVA and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF nanocomposite films towards volatile ammonia and acetic acid. The neat Gel/PVA film shows no color variation after exposure to volatile ammonia and acetic acid.

In contrast, Gel/PVA incorporated with RCA and MOF exhibits a very clear color change from pink to green after exposure to volatile ammonia for 15 min. The penetration of ammonia vapor into the paper resulted in hydrolysis, leading to the generation of hydroxyl ions and subsequent elevation of the indicator's pH, thereby inducing a color change. Furthermore, the indicator's original pink color turned pink in reaction to acetic acid vapor. The L, a, b and total color difference (ΔE) values of the indicator films to simulated solutions are also recorded to quantify the colorimetric response (Fig. 7e,f). The brightness (L-value) and yellowness (b-value) of the composite films show an increasing trend with exposure to ammonia, while the redness (a-value) declined. Meanwhile, it is also found that the ΔE value for both composite films changed, implying an obvious color variation that can be distinguished through the human eye. When exposed to acetic acid, the composite film's color change is insignificant, making it less sensitive to acetic acid vapors than alkaline vapors. The above findings demonstrate that the engineered composite film can detect ammonia gas released from seafood decomposition, making it a viable option for monitoring pH changes and freshness indicators of packaged seafood.

Shrimp, a highly perishable aquatic food, is prone to spoilage due to its high moisture content and the presence of unsaturated fatty acids and proteins. During storage, shrimp is susceptible to microbial contamination and internal protein breakdown, producing ammonia, biogenic amines, and higher pH values. These volatile compounds gradually accumulate in the package's headspace, which can be detected using ammonia-sensitive films [51]. From this perspective, shrimp was used as a real food sample to test formulated films' pH/ammonia sensor capability as intelligent packaging for food freshness detection. Fig. 8a shows the color changes of the fabricated films assembled in packaging cups during the 24 h shrimp storage at 28 °C. As expected, no color change was observed after storing the shrimp in the neat Gel/PVA film. In comparison, the color of the Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% changed from initial reddish/pink to light and dark green after 1 day, which was the critical point of spoilage of fresh shrimp and could be clearly distinguished by the human eye. The film's color parameters (Fig. 8b) and the shrimp's TVB-N and pH values (Fig. S2) were also measured at different times (0, 12, and 24 h) to evaluate the freshness of the shrimp with increased accuracy. The indicator's color shifting was quantitatively confirmed when the a-value and b-value dropped significantly and ΔE values increased. The pH and TVB-N values of shrimp on day 0 were 5.9 and 10.5 mg/100 g, and the pH and TVB-N increased to 7.1 and 30.4 mg/100 g with the extension of storage time. The increased pH value with time progressing is attributed to the formation of volatile nitrogen compounds by enzymes and microorganisms. Fresh shrimp has a TVB-N value of <20 mg/100, while metamorphic shrimp are known with a TVB-N value of >30 mg/100 g [74]. In this study, the TVB-N value of the shrimp gradually surpassed the upper threshold (30 mg/100 g), indicating that the sample underwent complete spoilage within 24 h and was deemed unsuitable for consumption. Then, there was a highly positive correlation between the color shifting of composite indicator film and TVB-N and pH of shrimps, indicating the non-destructive analytical capabilities of designed indicator films for quick, non-contact, and visual determination of real-time detection of seafood quality.

The composite films were applied in shrimp freshness preservation and monitoring due to their antibacterial ability and ammonia sensitivity. Shrimp samples were wrapped with Gel/PVA (control), Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1%, and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% while stored at 4 °C for 21 days. The pH, color parameters, and total viable bacteria count (TVC) of shrimps are reordered every 7 days to monitor the correlation between these spoilage factors and the appearance of shrimps and films (Fig. 9a-d). Shrimp become absolutely inedible above pH 7.7 [73]. The pH of shrimp on the initial day was 5.9 ± 0.02, and the pH value increased to 7.8 ± 0.01 at 21 days for shrimps wrapped with Gel/PVA, suggesting that the shrimp were spoiled and unsuitable for consumption. However, the pH values of shrimps packed with Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films were 7.3 ± 0.01 and 6.9 ± 0.01 at 21 days, which is less than the required standard limit even at long storage period. Moreover, TVC for the control group exceeded the threshold value of 7.0 log CFU/mL [74] after 7 days, unlike the shrimps wrapped in composite films, which affected customers' acceptance of the product and made a correct correlation with the increased pH. Over time during storage, the microbial breakdown of proteins produces volatile nitrogen compounds, leading to a subsequent rise in pH levels.

Furthermore, a color change from gray to red/yellow was observed after 7 days for the shrimps in control groups following spoiling. However, for the other groups, the color change gradually progressed from gray to pale yellow with an increased storage period. The shrimps' spoilage trend in different treated groups was also reflected by measuring the unpacked a-value of shrimps. The rate of change of the a-value (redness) of packaged shrimp was different depending on the type of packaging film. On the 21 days of storage, the a-value of shrimp wrapped with Gel/PVA film showed the highest increase in a value (from 1.6 to 10.5). In contrast, shrimp packaged with Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF1% and Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF3% films showed a slower rate of increase in a-values during storage, reaching 7.6 ± 0.4 and 6.9 ± 0.3 at the 21 days, respectively.

These results indicate that the Gel/PVA-packaged shrimps showed severe spoilage during storage due to severe color and pH changes and increased growth of food-eating bacteria. In comparison, shrimp packaged in composite indicator film showed no significant color, pH, and TVC changes until day 14 of the experiment, indicating an extended shrimp shelf life in these groups. The effect of Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF films on the extension of shrimp shelf life was attributed to the synergistic effect of the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of RCA and Cu-MOF.

However, the deterioration of shrimp in the composite group started gradually from the 14th day of storage. The Gel/PVA/RCA/MOFs films were pH-dependent and ammonia-sensitive due to the accumulation of volatile alkaline gases in the package headspace and showed color change after 21 days of storage. The composite film gradually changed from red to green on the 21st day, allowing customers to evaluate the quality of the product without opening the package. Therefore, the combined effect of anthocyanins and MOF nanostructures proved very effective in preserving shrimp freshness and showed countermeasures against shrimp spoilage.

A multifunctional, active, and intelligent packaging film was prepared by incorporating Cu-MOF particles and red cabbage anthocyanins into a gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol) blended biopolymer matrix. FT-IR and SEM results demonstrated the compatibility of the polymer backbone and fillers. Compared to the virgin Gel/PVA film, the composite film with 3 wt% MOF particles showed 100 % UV blocking and antioxidant activity, improved mechanical properties, and reduced WVP. The bactericidal effect against L. monocytogenes and E. coli was also observed for the developed MOF-added composite. The composite film can also be applied as a visual pH indicator because it exhibits a fast, visually detectable colorimetric response to pH changes (from 2 to 12) and volatile ammonia. In packaging tests, the double strain of the polymer matrix showed a color response to the level of product spoilage and delayed degradation of the packaged samples, confirming the composite indicator film's freshness preservation and monitoring capabilities. The multifunctional Gel/PVA/RCA/MOF films have high potential in smart food packaging applications.
